DESCRIPTION OF NIKE, INC.

bcbe9219-dfbe-4c8e-832d-a3b3c4c2b408.label: text_chunk
bcbe9219-dfbe-4c8e-832d-a3b3c4c2b408.text:
CAPITAL STOCK

The following description of Nike, Inc.’s (“Company”) capital stock is a summary that is not complete and is qualified in its entirety by reference to the Company’s Restated Articles of Incorporation (“Articles of Incorporation”) and the Company’s Fifth Restated By-Laws (“By ‑ Laws”).

General

The Company’s Articles of Incorporation provide that it may issue up to 400,000,000 shares of Class A Common Stock,

5117b0d5-2279-49b1-a95a-071b786df55c.label: text_chunk
5117b0d5-2279-49b1-a95a-071b786df55c.text: no par value (“Class A Common Stock”); 2,400,000,000 shares of Class B Common Stock, no par value (“Class B Common Stock” and collectively with the Class A Common Stock, the “Common Stock”); and 300,000 shares of Preferred Stock, par value $1.00 per share (“Preferred Stock”).

8695cbd1-7d65-4f08-a6ec-81bc40b4fec6.label: text_chunk
8695cbd1-7d65-4f08-a6ec-81bc40b4fec6.text: As of July 19, 2019, 315,024,752 shares of Class A Common Stock, 1,251,863,621 shares of Class B Common Stock and 300,000 shares of Preferred Stock were issued and outstanding.

a47fcfc0-13dc-4db0-b6f6-8e7163615ce5.label: text_chunk
a47fcfc0-13dc-4db0-b6f6-8e7163615ce5.text:
Voting and Other Rights

General

Each share of Common Stock is entitled to one vote per share, voting as a single class, on all matters which stockholders generally are entitled to vote, except as provided below in the case of voting for the election of directors. The holders of the Common Stock have no preemptive rights and are not entitled to cumulative voting.

5ab7e8f9-3db9-49cb-804c-2f96191d23cb.label: text_chunk
5ab7e8f9-3db9-49cb-804c-2f96191d23cb.text: Shares of Class A Common Stock are convertible into shares of Class B Common Stock on a share-for-share basis any time at the option of the holder, subject to certain terms and conditions. The Class B Common Stock does not have any conversion rights.

3c3dff1e-b0cf-4d17-aabb-b9db0a3b5460.label: text_chunk
3c3dff1e-b0cf-4d17-aabb-b9db0a3b5460.text: Except as otherwise required by law, holders of Preferred Stock do not have general voting rights but do have the right to vote as a separate class with one vote per share on, (i) the sale of all or substantially all of the assets of the Company and its subsidiaries, (ii) on a merger, consolidation, liquidation or dissolution of the Company, or (iii) on the sale or assignment of the “NIKE” trademark for athletic shoes sold in the United States.

514821b0-2c8a-480b-833e-1c0bc7d9bdd0.label: text_chunk
514821b0-2c8a-480b-833e-1c0bc7d9bdd0.text: If any such matter is submitted for approval by the holders of the Preferred Stock and is not approved by more than 66⅔% of such holders, then the Company has the right to redeem the shares of Preferred Stock voting against such action at the redemption price of $1.00 per share, plus accrued dividends and interest, if any, and the holders of such Preferred Stock have the right to require the Company to redeem their shares at the same price.

554291fb-44f5-4785-b45e-d3072704a4cf.label: text_chunk
554291fb-44f5-4785-b45e-d3072704a4cf.text: The outstanding shares of Preferred Stock may also be redeemed by the Company, at its option, or at the option of the holders of Preferred Stock, at a redemption price of $1.00 per share, plus any accrued dividends and interest, under certain other circumstances.

dd6e5581-1fa0-459f-a8a3-60b6ffa4f6ff.label: text_chunk
dd6e5581-1fa0-459f-a8a3-60b6ffa4f6ff.text:
Voting for the Election of Directors

At any time and so long as the number of outstanding shares of Class B Common Stock equals or exceeds 25% of the total outstanding shares of Common Stock, the holders of Class A Common Stock and Class B Common stock will vote as separate classes for the purposes of electing directors. In such case, holders of Class B Common Stock, voting as a separate class, shall be entitled to elect 25% (rounded up to

350cd64a-aa4d-4c2a-a7d5-5ceb58f1bd9e.label: text_chunk
350cd64a-aa4d-4c2a-a7d5-5ceb58f1bd9e.text: the nearest whole number) of the total number of authorized directors with holders of Class A Common Stock, voting as a separate class, electing the remaining directors.Notwithstanding the above paragraph, in the event that the number of outstanding shares of Class A Common Stock is less than 12.

80da7abf-57f9-4c2a-a166-9b4e9baaebac.label: text_chunk
80da7abf-57f9-4c2a-a166-9b4e9baaebac.text: 5% of the total number of Common Stock shares outstanding, the holders of Class B Common Stock, voting as a separate class, shall continue to elect 25% (rounded up to the nearest whole number) of the total number of authorized directors and the remaining directors will be elected by all holders of Common Stock voting together as a single class.

e83486fe-4488-4420-b5ae-3ef2318a3cca.label: text_chunk
e83486fe-4488-4420-b5ae-3ef2318a3cca.text:
If the number of outstanding shares of Class B Common Stock is less than 25% of the total number of Common Stock shares outstanding, the holders of the Common Stock will elect all directors voting together as a single class.

Dividend and Liquidation Rights

Dividends on the Preferred Stock, which are preferential and cumulative, are at the rate of $0.10 per share per annum.

a75d9629-27e0-4b1d-a8dd-d911fb3a07f3.label: text_chunk
a75d9629-27e0-4b1d-a8dd-d911fb3a07f3.text: Any unpaid dividends on the Preferred Stock accrue interest at the rate of 12% per annum.The holders of Class A Common Stock and Class B Common Stock are entitled to share ratably in dividends when and as declared by the Company’s Board of Directors.

606c1b49-5e8b-46fd-b148-89b9ddee24fc.label: text_chunk
606c1b49-5e8b-46fd-b148-89b9ddee24fc.text: Dividends on the Common Stock may not be paid in any fiscal year unless all dividends for such fiscal year on all outstanding shares of Preferred Stock have been declared and paid, or set apart for payment, in full.Holders of Class A and Class B Common Stock also are entitled to share ratably in any proceeds legally available for distribution to shareholders upon liquidation or dissolution, after provision is first made for holders of the Preferred Stock.

a4147ea6-f839-488f-97ef-7bb865848d01.label: text_chunk
a4147ea6-f839-488f-97ef-7bb865848d01.text:
Holders of Preferred Stock are entitled to receive $1.00 per share, together with dividends declared and unpaid, prior to any payment upon liquidation to holders of the outstanding Common Stock.

Anti-takeover Effects of Certain Provisions of the Certificate of Incorporation and By-Laws

The provisions of the Company’s Articles of Incorporation and By-Laws summarized below may have an anti-takeover effect and may delay, defer or prevent a tender offer or takeover attempt.

896dc474-7315-4907-bf15-d26f152b4206.label: text_chunk
896dc474-7315-4907-bf15-d26f152b4206.text:
Voting Thresholds

The Company’s Articles of Incorporation provide that a vote of 80% of all of the outstanding Common Stock, voting together as one class, is required to approve a merger, sale of assets or similar business transaction with any beneficial holder of 10% or more of the Company’s Common Stock, unless the transaction is first approved by the Board of Directors.

c1fcaa05-7e58-4702-97b5-84e54e44de2c.label: text_chunk
c1fcaa05-7e58-4702-97b5-84e54e44de2c.text: The 80% vote in such instance must include the affirmative vote of at least 50% of the Common Stock held by persons other than the interested party. Additionally, any amendment of provisions of the Articles of Incorporation concerning the 80% voting provisions, the number of directors constituting the Board of Directors, and the removal of directors, also requires the approval of the holders of 80% of the outstanding Common Stock.

66543764-e7b1-4ff8-a8f9-94090439b9ee.label: text_chunk
66543764-e7b1-4ff8-a8f9-94090439b9ee.text:
These provisions with respect to super-majority voting requirements may make it more difficult for any person interested in acquiring the Company to effect a merger or other business combination with the Company. Any shareholder or group of shareholders owning more than 20% of the Common Stock
will have effective veto power over certain proposed acquisitions of the Company.

b1f36576-d223-4357-924d-0dbc80947236.label: text_chunk
b1f36576-d223-4357-924d-0dbc80947236.text: Such provisions could result in the holders of a majority of the outstanding voting Common Stock being unable to effect requisite shareholder approval, even though the terms of such combination might be considered favorable.

285a40b6-bfcb-40a3-b621-f2a19f58a722.label: text_chunk
285a40b6-bfcb-40a3-b621-f2a19f58a722.text:
Authorized but Unissued Common Stock

The Company’s shares of authorized and unissued common stock will be available for future issuance without additional stockholders approval, provided that that the Company shall at all times keep available enough shares of Class B Common Stock as would be needed to convert all outstanding shares of Class A Common Stock to Class B Common Stock on a share-for-share basis.

a2636636-df46-498b-8082-cf67c181aae7.label: text_chunk
a2636636-df46-498b-8082-cf67c181aae7.text:
The existence of authorized but unissued shares of Common Stock may enable the Company’s Board of Directors to render more difficult or to discourage an attempt to obtain control of the Company by means of a merger, tender offer or otherwise.

No Cumulative Voting

The Articles of Incorporation do not grant holders of the Common Stock the right to vote cumulatively.

cf8cba73-8399-4435-873f-f2e2761f0989.label: text_chunk
cf8cba73-8399-4435-873f-f2e2761f0989.text:
Notice Provisions Relating to Stockholder Proposals and Nominees

The Company’s By-Laws contain provisions requiring stockholders to give advance written notice to the Company of a proposal or director nomination in order to have the proposal or the nominee considered at an annual meeting of stockholders. The notice for a shareholder proposal must be received not less than 60 days prior to the meeting.

4717f6b7-48ac-4c4e-95df-fe7453ed25cd.label: text_chunk
4717f6b7-48ac-4c4e-95df-fe7453ed25cd.text:
Market Listing

The Company’s Class B Common Stock is listed under the New York Stock Exchange under the symbol “NKE”.

Transfer Agents and Registrar

The transfer agent and registrar for the Company’s Common Stock is Computershare Trust Company, Inc..

3. **Carrying Amount and Fair Value**: The carrying amount of an asset group is compared to its fair value to assess potential impairment, emphasizing the importance of valuation in financial reporting.

5. **U.S. Statutory Federal Income Tax Rate and Effective Income Tax Rate**: The statutory rate serves as a benchmark for the effective income tax rate, which reflects the actual tax burden after accounting for deductions and credits, illustrating the relationship between theoretical tax rates and practical tax liabilities.

6. **May 31, 2018, and May 31, 2017**: This comparison focuses on the effective tax rates for these two years, providing insights into changes in tax burdens over time.

   - **Investor Relations**: NIKE communicates regularly with investors and securities analysts, filing reports with the SEC to ensure transparency and compliance with federal securities laws.

The relationships outlined highlight various critical interactions between entities in the economic and financial landscape. Here’s a summary of the key relationships:

1. **Global Economic Environment and Currency Exchange Rates**: The global economic environment significantly influences currency exchange rates, which in turn affects international trade and financial transactions.

2. **Mergers and Acquisitions and Market Position**: Mergers and acquisitions can alter a company's market position by reshaping the competitive landscape and reallocating resources.

3. **Proposals and Cash Flows**: Legislative proposals, if enacted, may adversely impact a company's cash flows by changing its tax obligations.

4. **Counterparty Financial Institutions and Business**: The stability of counterparty financial institutions is crucial for businesses, as defaults can lead to substantial financial losses.

5. **Demand and Materials**: An increase in consumer demand necessitates a higher requirement for materials, driving production needs.

6. **Transportation and Manufacturers**: Efficient transportation is vital for manufacturers, directly affecting their product delivery capabilities and overall operational performance.

7. **Oil and Petroleum Products**: Fluctuations in oil prices directly influence the pricing of petroleum products, which are derived from oil.

8. **Economic Instability and Financial Results**: Economic instability can negatively impact financial results by reducing consumer spending and investment, leading to decreased profitability.

9. **Income Tax Expense and Income Before Income Taxes**: Changes in income before income taxes directly affect the calculation of income tax expense.

10. **Foreign Currency-Related Gains and Losses and Income Before Income Taxes**: Gains and losses from foreign currency transactions are included in the calculation of income before income taxes, impacting overall profitability.

11. **Performance and Endorsers**: The performance of endorsers affects their payment structures as specified in contracts.

12. **Accumulated Other Comprehensive Income and Net Income**: Changes in the fair value of hedge contracts are recorded in accumulated other comprehensive income until they affect net income.

13. **Hedge Derivatives and Accumulated Other Comprehensive Income**: Gains and losses from hedge derivatives are recorded in accumulated other comprehensive income prior to impacting net income.

14. **Interest Rates and Earnings**: Interest rates influence earnings by affecting borrowing costs and overall financial performance.

15. **Cumulative-Effect Adjustment and Retained Earnings**: The cumulative-effect adjustment modifies retained earnings by reflecting the effects of new accounting standards.

16. **Current Period Tax Positions and Unrecognized Tax Benefits**: Current tax positions can directly influence the amount of unrecognized tax benefits, leading to potential increases or decreases.

17. **Cash Flow Hedges and Accumulated Other Comprehensive Income**: Changes in the fair value of cash flow hedges are recorded in accumulated other comprehensive income until they impact net income.

This summary encapsulates the interconnectedness of economic factors and their implications for businesses, highlighting the importance of understanding these relationships for strategic decision-making.

The relationships summarized are centered around Topic 606, a significant standard in revenue recognition. 

1. **Topic 606 and Topic 605**: Topic 606 is the successor to Topic 605, indicating that it has replaced Topic 605 as the current standard for revenue recognition.

2. **Development of Topic 606**: Topic 606 was developed by the Financial Accounting Standards Board (FASB) to provide updated guidance on revenue recognition, replacing previous standards.

3. **Impact on Financial Reporting**: The adoption of Topic 606 establishes a framework for recognizing revenue, which influences how revenues are reported in the Consolidated Statements of Income. However, it is noted that this change did not have a material effect on fiscal 2019 reporting.

In summary, Topic 606, developed by the FASB, serves as the new standard for revenue recognition, succeeding Topic 605 and guiding the reporting of revenues in financial statements.

1. **Oversight and Directors**: Directors are responsible for overseeing the activities of Executive Officers. This oversight ensures that the management of the company operates in alignment with the interests of shareholders, highlighting the Directors' role in corporate governance.

2. **Audit Committee and Board of Directors**: The Audit Committee is a subset of the Board of Directors, indicating a hierarchical relationship. The Audit Committee reports to the Board, emphasizing its accountability and the importance of financial oversight within the broader governance framework.

In summary, the relationships illustrate a structured governance system where Directors oversee Executive Officers to protect shareholder interests, while the Audit Committee functions under the Board of Directors to ensure accountability and transparency in financial matters.

The relationships outlined in the knowledge graph highlight various interactions between entities in the context of retail, commerce, finance, and accounting. Here’s a summary of the key relationships:

1. **Retail Space and Operating Lease Commitments**: Companies often enter into operating lease commitments to utilize retail spaces for their operations, indicating a significant financial obligation tied to commercial property usage.

2. **Retail Store Customers and Transaction Price**: Retail store customers play a crucial role in determining the transaction price, as they purchase goods or services, which is reflected in the invoiced sales price at the point of sale.

3. **Digital Commerce Customers and Transaction Price**: Similar to retail store customers, digital commerce customers are involved in the transaction price through their online purchases, with the price being relevant upon shipment of the goods.

4. **Wholesale Transactions and Customer**: In wholesale transactions, customers are entities that purchase goods in bulk from sellers, highlighting a different market dynamic compared to retail sales.

5. **Tax Payments and Cash**: Tax payments are settled using cash, representing a direct financial transaction where cash is used to fulfill tax obligations, emphasizing the importance of liquidity in financial management.

6. **Level 2 and Liabilities**: Level 2 inputs are utilized to assess the value of liabilities, providing essential observable data that influences their valuation in financial reporting, which is critical for accurate financial statements.

This summary encapsulates the nature and significance of the relationships, illustrating how entities interact within the realms of retail, digital commerce, finance, and accounting.

### Summary of Relationships

1. **Sales and Profit Margins**: Sales levels directly influence profit margins, with higher sales potentially leading to increased profitability when costs are effectively managed.

2. **Retail Economy and Financial Condition**: A weakening retail economy negatively impacts the financial condition of businesses, resulting in lower sales and heightened financial risks for customers.

3. **Intellectual Property Rights and Business**: The protection of intellectual property rights is essential for businesses, as it affects their operational capabilities and competitive positioning in the market.

4. **U.S. Tax Laws and Tax Cuts and Jobs Act**: The Tax Cuts and Jobs Act has significantly altered U.S. tax laws, changing the application and collection of taxes.

5. **Information Technology Problems and Distribution Facilities**: Issues in information technology can disrupt the operations of distribution facilities, indicating a critical dependency that can affect overall business performance.

6. **Macroeconomic Conditions and Debt**: Macroeconomic factors can hinder the management and refinancing of debt, impacting borrowers' financial obligations.

7. **Changes and Consolidated Results of Operations**: Certain changes have been assessed as having no impact on the consolidated results of operations, suggesting a stable relationship with financial performance metrics.

8. **Other Income and Foreign Currency Conversion Gains and Losses**: Variations in Other Income are primarily linked to changes in Foreign Currency Conversion Gains and Losses, highlighting a direct financial relationship.

9. **Foreign Exchange Fluctuations and U.S. Dollar**: Fluctuations in foreign exchange rates can adversely affect investments in currencies other than the U.S. Dollar, emphasizing the relationship between currency volatility and the U.S. Dollar's value.

10. **International Subsidiaries and Foreign Currency Translation Adjustments**: International subsidiaries are influenced by foreign currency translation adjustments, which can affect the parent company's financial reporting.

11. **Foreign Currency Rate Fluctuations and Derivative Instruments**: Fluctuations in foreign currency rates can significantly impact the value and performance of derivative instruments, which are often used as hedges against such fluctuations.

12. **Property, Plant and Equipment and Deferred Tax Liabilities**: Property, Plant and Equipment can result in Deferred Tax Liabilities due to depreciation and timing differences in expense recognition for tax versus accounting purposes.

This summary encapsulates the critical relationships between various entities and their implications for business performance, financial conditions, and operational challenges.

The relationships outlined in the knowledge graph highlight the connections between financial reporting concepts and regulatory guidance. 

1. **Retained Earnings and ASU 2018-02**: Retained Earnings are influenced by ASU 2018-02, which offers guidance on reclassifying specific tax effects from accumulated other comprehensive income to retained earnings. This reclassification impacts the overall balance of retained earnings.

2. **Consolidated Balance Sheets and Topic 606**: The preparation of Consolidated Balance Sheets is guided by Topic 606, which dictates the recognition of revenue and related reserves, thereby shaping how financial performance is reported.

3. **Consolidated Balance Sheets and U.S. GAAP**: The Consolidated Balance Sheets must comply with U.S. GAAP, ensuring adherence to the established accounting standards in the United States.

4. **Compliance with U.S. GAAP**: The requirement for Consolidated Balance Sheets to comply with U.S. GAAP reinforces the importance of following standardized accounting practices.

Overall, these relationships emphasize the critical role of regulatory frameworks, such as ASU 2018-02 and Topic 606, in guiding the preparation and presentation of financial statements, ensuring accuracy and compliance with accounting standards.

### Summary of Relationships

1. **Research, Design and Development -> Customer**: Efforts in research and development aim to enhance products and services, improving customer satisfaction and sales.

2. **Global Economic Conditions -> Financial Results**: Economic fluctuations significantly affect financial outcomes, influencing both revenues and expenses.

3. **Trade Protection Measures -> Suppliers**: Trade restrictions can limit product availability from suppliers, necessitating alternative sourcing strategies.

4. **Product Innovation -> Brand Image**: Innovations in products can positively shape brand perception and consumer satisfaction.

5. **Lead Times -> Products**: The duration of lead times affects product availability, complicating market responsiveness.

6. **Teams -> Brand Image**: The reputation of teams can enhance or damage brand image through their endorsements and actions.

7. **Economic Conditions -> Consumer Preferences**: Shifts in economic conditions can alter consumer purchasing behaviors and preferences.

8. **Inventory Levels -> Customer Demand**: Inventory levels are directly tied to customer demand, with excess or shortages impacting supply chain efficiency.

9. **Inventory Shortages -> Customers**: Shortages can delay shipments, adversely affecting customer satisfaction and business relationships.

10. **Administration -> Reputation**: Public statements by administration regarding multinational corporations can harm their reputation, impacting customer trust.

11. **Retail Stores -> Operating Results**: Effective management of retail stores is crucial for profitability and overall operating results.

12. **Economic Environment -> Real Estate Properties**: The economic landscape influences real estate valuations and rental rates, affecting investment choices.

13. **Extreme Weather Conditions -> Retail Stores**: Severe weather can disrupt retail operations, leading to sales losses and diminished business performance.

14. **Internal Controls -> Financial Reporting Obligations**: Strong internal controls are essential for fulfilling financial reporting requirements; inadequacies can lead to reporting challenges.

15. **Estimates and Assumptions -> Financial Statements**: Management's estimates and assumptions directly impact reported financial figures.

16. **Monetary Assets -> Foreign Currency Conversion Gains**: Fluctuations in currency values can result in gains or losses for monetary assets upon re-measurement.

17. **Monetary Liabilities -> Foreign Currency Conversion Gains**: Similar to assets, monetary liabilities are affected by currency conversion, influencing overall financial outcomes.

18. **Net Foreign Currency Gains and Losses -> Monetary Assets and Liabilities**: Currency fluctuations directly impact the net gains and losses associated with monetary assets and liabilities.

19. **Long-Term Debt Ratings -> Interest Rate**: Improved debt ratings can lower borrowing costs, reflecting reduced risk for lenders.

20. **Actual Returns -> Net Revenues**: Actual returns are deducted from total sales, directly affecting net revenue calculations.

21. **Discounts -> Net Revenues**: Offering discounts reduces product prices, consequently decreasing net revenues.

22. **Total Selling and Administrative Expense -> Net Income**: Selling and administrative expenses directly influence net income calculations.

23. **Net Income -> Stock-based Compensation**: Stock-based compensation is an expense that reduces net income, highlighting their direct relationship.

24. **Tax Laws -> Corporate Tax Rate**: Tax legislation establishes the framework for corporate tax rates, directly influencing taxation levels.

25. **Net Income Available to Common Stockholders -> Weighted Average Common Shares Outstanding**: This net income figure is essential for calculating earnings per share (EPS).

26. **Tax (Expense) Benefit -> Gain (Loss) Net of Tax**: Tax benefits affect net gains or losses by reducing overall tax liabilities.

This summary encapsulates the interconnected nature of various business relationships, emphasizing how changes in one area can significantly impact others, ultimately affecting overall performance and stakeholder perceptions.

community_summary.349:
The relationships summarized involve the concept of "Diluted Earnings Per Common Share" (DEPS) and its calculation methodology. 

1. **Diluted Earnings Per Common Share (DEPS)** is calculated using the **Weighted Average Outstanding Shares**. This calculation adjusts the weighted average to account for potential dilution from stock options and awards, ensuring a more accurate representation of earnings per share.

2. The calculation of DEPS **excludes Options**, as these are deemed anti-dilutive. This means that when options are not likely to be exercised or would not reduce the earnings per share, they are not included in the calculation.

Overall, the key entities involved are "Diluted Earnings Per Common Share," "Weighted Average Outstanding Shares," and "Options," with the primary focus on how DEPS is derived and the rationale for excluding certain elements from its calculation.
community_summary.350:
The relationships outlined involve the financial instrument known as Interest Rate Swaps and their applications in managing interest costs and hedging long-term debt. 

1. **Interest Rate Swaps and Interest Expense**: Interest Rate Swaps are employed to manage and potentially reduce Interest Expense. This is achieved by exchanging fixed interest payments for variable ones, or the other way around, allowing companies to optimize their interest costs.

2. **Interest Rate Swaps and Long-term Debt**: These swaps are also utilized to hedge against risks related to fluctuations in the fair value of Long-term Debt. This hedging strategy helps companies mitigate potential losses that could arise from interest rate changes.

In summary, Interest Rate Swaps serve a dual purpose: they are instrumental in managing interest expenses and provide a protective measure against the volatility of long-term debt valuations.

community_summary.364:
**Summary of Relationships Involving NIKE, INC.**

1. **Annual Meeting of Shareholders**: NIKE, INC. hosts this event, showcasing its role as the organizing entity for shareholder engagement.

2. **Customer Relations**: NIKE, INC. handles both transactional and personal information about its customers, establishing a direct relationship with individuals who purchase its products.

3. **Cybersecurity Threats**: The company faces threats from hackers, highlighting the risks to its data security and privacy.

4. **Tax Obligations**: NIKE, INC. is subject to income taxes as a corporation, necessitating compliance with tax regulations and accurate reporting of tax expenses.

5. **Revenue Generation**: The company generates revenue through its NIKE Brand, which encompasses various product categories, including footwear, apparel, and equipment.

6. **Financial Performance**: NIKE, INC. reports financial metrics such as 'Income Before Income Taxes,' reflecting its earnings after all expenses except for income tax.

7. **Currency Operations**: A portion of NIKE, INC.'s revenues is generated in British Pounds, indicating its operational ties to European markets.

8. **Credit Facilities**: NIKE, INC. has access to borrow up to $2 billion through a committed credit facility agreement, with a maturity date set for August 28, 2020.

9. **Financial Reporting**: The company is responsible for preparing its Consolidated Financial Statements, which are influenced by its accounting policies and reflect its financial health and shareholder equity.

10. **Cost Management**: NIKE, INC. incurs costs of sales, including shipping and handling, as part of its business operations.

11. **Regulatory Compliance**: The company must adhere to accounting standards set by the FASB, which affects its financial reporting, including metrics like retained earnings and deferred income taxes.

12. **Global Operations**: NIKE, INC. operates in foreign markets, contributing to its income before income taxes and emphasizing its global presence.

13. **Liabilities**: The company has unfunded pension liabilities classified under deferred income taxes and other liabilities, indicating financial obligations related to its pension plans.

14. **Currency Risk Management**: NIKE, INC. employs hedging strategies to manage foreign currency exposures related to product costs and revenues denominated in U.S. Dollars.

15. **Audit Engagement**: NIKE, INC. engages an Independent Registered Public Accounting Firm for auditing and financial reporting, as noted in the Proxy Statement for the Annual Meeting of Shareholders.

This summary encapsulates the key relationships and operational aspects of NIKE, INC., emphasizing its corporate governance, financial health, customer engagement, and global operations.
community_summary.365:
The relationships summarized involve two primary entities: **Dividends Payable** and **Item 403**, both of which are integral components of broader financial and governance documents.

1. **Dividends Payable** is categorized as a type of liability that is included in the **TOTAL Accrued Liabilities** of a company. This indicates that dividends payable contribute to the overall financial obligations that a company must account for.

2. **Item 403** is a specific section within the **Proxy Statement**. It mandates the disclosure of essential information related to the election of directors and stock holdings, highlighting its importance in corporate governance and transparency.

In summary, Dividends Payable is a significant liability affecting a company's financial statements, while Item 403 serves a critical role in ensuring that shareholders are informed about key governance matters in the Proxy Statement.

The relationships outlined highlight the distinctions and significance of Common Stock and Preferred Stock as forms of equity securities, along with the rights and powers associated with Common Stock ownership.

1. **Common Stock and Preferred Stock**: Both are types of equity securities that signify ownership in a company, yet they differ in rights and claims on assets. Common Stock includes Class A and Class B stocks, while Preferred Stock is categorized differently, typically having a par value.

2. **Dividends**: Common stockholders are entitled to receive dividends, which serve as a return on their investment. This entitlement is a primary advantage of holding Common Stock.

3. **Voting Rights**: Holders of Common Stock collectively vote to elect directors, thereby influencing the board's composition. This voting power underscores the role of Common Stockholders in corporate governance.

4. **Ownership Influence**: Shareholders with more than 20% ownership of Common Stock possess effective veto power over certain corporate acquisitions, illustrating how stock ownership can directly impact corporate decisions.

In summary, Common Stock provides shareholders with voting rights and dividend entitlements, while also allowing significant influence over corporate governance and strategic decisions, distinguishing it from Preferred Stock.

The relationships between Class A Common Stock and Class B Common Stock highlight key aspects of corporate governance, financial interactions, and shareholder rights. 

1. **Conversion**: Class A Common Stock can be converted into Class B Common Stock, which alters ownership percentages. This illustrates a significant relationship based on stock classification and the potential for shareholders to change their investment structure.

2. **Electing the Board of Directors**: Holders of Class A Common Stock have the authority to elect three-quarters of the Board of Directors. This emphasizes the governance power associated with Class A shares, showcasing their influence in corporate decision-making.

3. **Convertible Nature**: The ability for Class A Common Stock to be converted into Class B Common Stock upon the holder's request indicates a direct financial relationship, allowing flexibility for investors in managing their stock holdings.

4. **Voting Rights**: Both Class A and Class B Common Stocks participate in the voting process for electing directors, although they do so as distinct classes. This separation underscores the different rights and privileges associated with each stock class.

5. **Dividend Entitlement**: Holders of both Class A and Class B Common Stock are entitled to share in dividends declared by the Board of Directors, reflecting a shared financial benefit that aligns the interests of both classes of shareholders.

Overall, these relationships illustrate the complexities of stock classification, governance rights, and financial entitlements within the corporate structure, emphasizing the distinct roles and privileges of Class A and Class B Common Stock.

The relationships outlined in the knowledge graph highlight the interconnectedness of Other Comprehensive Income (OCI), Reclassification, and Retained Earnings within financial reporting. 

1. **Other Comprehensive Income and Net Income**: Other Comprehensive Income can include gains and losses that may be reclassified to Net Income in future periods. This indicates that fluctuations in OCI can directly influence Net Income, showcasing the dynamic nature of financial performance reporting.

2. **Reclassification and Retained Earnings**: The reclassification of stranded tax effects from Accumulated Other Comprehensive Income (loss) to Retained Earnings illustrates a direct influence where changes in OCI impact Retained Earnings. This relationship emphasizes the importance of accurate financial reporting and the implications of OCI on overall equity.

3. **Accumulated Other Comprehensive Income (Loss) and Location of Gain (Loss)**: The location of gain or loss is derived from the Accumulated Other Comprehensive Income (loss), indicating a transition of financial elements from one accounting category to another. This relationship underscores the significance of tracking OCI for understanding the sources of gains and losses.

Overall, these relationships collectively illustrate how Other Comprehensive Income, through processes of reclassification, plays a crucial role in shaping Net Income and Retained Earnings, thereby affecting the overall financial health and reporting of an entity.
